content.text: Token IDs are non-negative integers that uniquely identify each token in a tokenizer's vocabulary. When a string is tokenized, the output is a sequence of these integers — not the string characters, not the token subword strings themselves, but compact integer indices into the vocabulary table. A sentence like 'The cat sat on the mat' becomes a sequence such as [464, 3797, 3332, 319, 262, 2603] under GPT-2's tokenizer, where each integer is a key into a lookup table of 50,257 entries.
Embeddings are dense, low-dimensional vector representations of these token IDs. The embedding matrix
maps each token ID to a real-valued vector of dimension
(commonly 768, 1024, 2048, or 4096 for modern models). The embedding for token
. These vectors are learned parameters — they are initialized randomly and updated via gradient descent during pretraining to encode semantic and syntactic regularities of language.

Together, token IDs and embeddings form the input bridge between discrete symbolic text and the continuous vector space in which transformer computations occur. The tokenizer converts raw text to IDs; the embedding layer converts IDs to vectors; the transformer processes vectors; the output layer projects back to vocabulary-sized logit vectors; the tokenizer then converts the predicted IDs back to text. Understanding this pipeline is essential for working with language models at any level of depth.

Token IDs and embeddings are the interface layer between the symbolic world of text and the numerical world of deep learning. Every downstream property of a language model — what concepts it can distinguish, how it generalizes across words, what biases it encodes — is fundamentally determined by how well the embedding space organizes meaning.

In practice, understanding this layer matters for several engineering reasons. When adapting models, adding new domain-specific tokens requires adding rows to the embedding matrix, but those rows start randomly initialized and need training data to become useful. When debugging model behavior, inspecting embedding similarity can reveal whether the model treats two tokens as semantically related. When optimizing inference, the embedding lookup is a memory-bound operation whose bandwidth is a performance bottleneck on GPUs with small batch sizes.

For agentic AI systems specifically, token IDs determine how the model perceives tool names, function signatures, and structured data formats. A tool named 'search_web' might tokenize to ['search', '_web'] or ['search', '_', 'web'] depending on the tokenizer, affecting how the model's attention patterns learn to recognize and invoke it. Designing agent interfaces with tokenization-friendly naming conventions is a practical engineering concern.

A 1,024-dimensional embedding vector requires 4 KB of memory (float32). A token ID is 4 bytes. The ID-to-embedding lookup is a single GEMM-free memory read — far cheaper than computing token representations on the fly.

Training drives related tokens to nearby embedding vectors. Classic examples: king - man + woman ≈ queen; Paris - France + Germany ≈ Berlin. This geometry enables the model to generalize across morphological variants, synonyms, and semantic categories.

Embeddings are continuous real vectors, so gradients flow through them during training. The same backpropagation that updates attention weights also updates embeddings, tying the representation to the model's overall language objective.

Most modern language models share the embedding matrix between the input embedding layer and the output projection layer. This halves the parameter count for the vocabulary-related weights and helps the model learn consistent representations of tokens across input and output positions.

Pretrained embeddings capture world knowledge encoded in the pretraining corpus. Fine-tuning starts from this rich initialization rather than random vectors, requiring far less data to reach task-specific accuracy.

The embedding matrix grows with vocabulary size and embedding dimension. GPT-3's 50,257-token vocabulary with d=12,288 requires 50,257 × 12,288 × 4 bytes ≈ 2.5 GB for just the embedding parameters — about 15% of the total model size.
New tokens added to the vocabulary are initialized randomly. They require substantial fine-tuning data before their embeddings capture meaningful semantics.

Token IDs are opaque integers with no intrinsic meaning. Debugging pipelines that log token IDs without decoding them is difficult, and ID-based comparisons (e.g., 'does the output contain token 1234?') are brittle across model versions.

The embedding matrix is trained for a specific tokenizer vocabulary. Changing the tokenizer — even to one with the same vocabulary size but different token assignments — invalidates all learned embeddings.

For batch sizes of 1 (common in chatbot serving), the embedding lookup reads only 1 row out of 50,000 from the matrix. This wastes 99.998% of the loaded cache lines and is memory-bandwidth bound, not compute-bound.

The tokenizer maintains a vocabulary file that maps token strings to integer IDs. The mapping is a bijection: each unique token string has exactly one ID, and each ID maps to exactly one string. IDs are assigned in order of addition to the vocabulary: the initial character set gets the lowest IDs, and each new merge rule produces the next sequential ID. Special tokens are typically assigned IDs at the beginning (0–4) or end of the vocabulary.
The embedding layer is implemented as a matrix multiply with a one-hot encoded input. For a token with ID
In practice, this is implemented as a direct memory lookup (indexed read) rather than a matrix multiply, because the one-hot encoding is sparse. PyTorch's
module implements this as an index into the weight tensor. The resulting vector is a 1D float array of size

Raw token embeddings carry no positional information — a sequence with the same tokens in different orders produces the same set of embedding vectors. Positional encodings are added to the embeddings before the transformer blocks to inject position information. Common approaches are:
Learned absolute positions (GPT-2, BERT): a learned matrix
is the positional encoding for position

Rotary position embeddings (RoPE, LLaMA): applied inside each attention head to query and key vectors, rotating them by an angle proportional to position. This allows extrapolation beyond the training context length.
After the final transformer layer, the hidden state at each position is a vector of dimension
. To generate probabilities over the vocabulary, this vector is projected by the output matrix
to produce a logit vector of dimension
— the same matrix that performs the input embedding. This constraint is a form of regularization that significantly reduces parameters and has been found to improve perplexity on most benchmarks.
The diagram below shows the complete pipeline from raw text to output probabilities, with the embedding matrix playing a dual role as input lookup and output projection.

attrs.alt: Token IDs to embeddings to logits pipeline diagram

content.text: After tokenization converts raw text to integer IDs, each ID is looked up as a row in the embedding matrix E. The resulting vectors are summed with positional encodings to produce the input to the first transformer block. After L transformer blocks, the final hidden states are projected back through E's transpose to produce logit vectors, which are softmax-normalized to give next-token probabilities.
Understand token IDs and embeddings when debugging unexpected model behavior — checking whether two tokens are near each other in embedding space can explain why the model treats them similarly.
Work at the embedding level when implementing retrieval-augmented systems: embedding similarity (cosine distance) between query and document embeddings determines retrieval quality.

Add new tokens to the vocabulary when fine-tuning on a domain with many out-of-vocabulary terms (medical codes, product SKUs, specialized syntax). Initialize new embeddings from the mean of related existing embeddings rather than randomly.
Access intermediate embeddings (not just output logits) for tasks like sentence similarity, semantic search, and classification using the [CLS] embedding or mean pooling of all token embeddings.
Avoid training embeddings for classification tasks on small datasets: freeze the embedding layer and train only the task head to prevent overfitting.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SimpleEmbedding(nn.Module):
    """Minimal embedding + positional encoding layer."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        vocab_size: int,
        d_model: int,
        max_seq_len: int = 2048,
        dropout: float = 0.1,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        # Token embedding matrix: shape (vocab_size, d_model)
        self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        # Learned positional embedding: shape (max_seq_len, d_model)
        self.position_embedding = nn.Embedding(max_seq_len, d_model)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.d_model = d_model

        # Weight tying: share token embedding with output projection
        # (set after creating the output layer)

    def forward(self, token_ids: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Args:
            token_ids: (batch_size, seq_len) integer tensor
        Returns:
            embeddings: (batch_size, seq_len, d_model) float tensor
        """
        batch_size, seq_len = token_ids.shape
        positions = torch.arange(seq_len, device=token_ids.device).unsqueeze(0)
        # positions shape: (1, seq_len), broadcasts across batch

        tok_emb = self.token_embedding(token_ids)          # (B, T, d)
        pos_emb = self.position_embedding(positions)        # (1, T, d)
        return self.dropout(tok_emb + pos_emb)             # (B, T, d)


# Example usage
vocab_size = 50_257  # GPT-2 vocabulary
d_model = 768        # GPT-2 small
model = SimpleEmbedding(vocab_size, d_model)

# Simulate a batch of 2 sequences of length 16
token_ids = torch.randint(0, vocab_size, (2, 16))
output = model(token_ids)
print("Output shape:", output.shape)   # torch.Size([2, 16, 768])

# Inspect embedding for a specific token
the_id = 464  # GPT-2 token ID for "The"
the_embedding = model.token_embedding.weight[the_id]
print("'The' embedding shape:", the_embedding.shape)  # torch.Size([768])
print("'The' embedding norm:", the_embedding.norm().item())
Extracting Token Embeddings from a Pretrained Model

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel

# Load BERT (produces token-level embeddings from final hidden states)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model.eval()

# Encode text
text = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# outputs.last_hidden_state: (batch, seq_len, 768)
last_hidden = outputs.last_hidden_state
print("Hidden states shape:", last_hidden.shape)

# Token IDs and their corresponding embeddings
token_ids = inputs["input_ids"][0].tolist()
tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(token_ids)

print("\nToken → ID → Embedding norm:")
for tok, tid, emb in zip(tokens, token_ids, last_hidden[0]):
    print(f"  {tok:<15} {tid:>6}  ||v|| = {emb.norm().item():.3f}")

# Sentence embedding via [CLS] token (position 0)
cls_embedding = last_hidden[0][0]  # shape (768,)
print(f"\nSentence embedding (CLS) shape: {cls_embedding.shape}")

# Or use mean pooling over all non-padding tokens
mask = inputs["attention_mask"][0].bool()
mean_embedding = last_hidden[0][mask].mean(dim=0)
print(f"Mean-pooled embedding shape: {mean_embedding.shape}")

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch.nn.functional as F

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model.eval()

def get_embedding(text: str) -> torch.Tensor:
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, max_length=512)
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)
    # Mean pool over token embeddings, excluding [CLS] and [SEP]
    hidden = outputs.last_hidden_state[0][1:-1]
    return F.normalize(hidden.mean(dim=0), dim=0)

# Compare token embedding cosine similarities
words = ["king", "queen", "man", "woman", "France", "Paris", "Germany", "Berlin"]
embeddings = {w: get_embedding(w) for w in words}

def cosine_sim(a: str, b: str) -> float:
    return (embeddings[a] @ embeddings[b]).item()

print("Semantic similarity (cosine):")
pairs = [
    ("king", "queen"), ("man", "woman"),
    ("France", "Paris"), ("Germany", "Berlin"),
    ("king", "France"), ("man", "Paris"),  # unrelated
]
for a, b in pairs:
    sim = cosine_sim(a, b)
    print(f"  {a:<10} ↔ {b:<10}  {sim:.3f}")

# Verify analogy: king - man + woman ≈ queen
analogy = embeddings["king"] - embeddings["man"] + embeddings["woman"]
analogy = F.normalize(analogy, dim=0)
sims = {w: (analogy @ embeddings[w]).item() for w in words}
top = sorted(sims.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:3]
print("\nking - man + woman ≈:", [f"{w}({s:.3f})" for w, s in top])

In production semantic search systems — used by GitHub Copilot, Bing, Notion AI, and many others — the key operation is computing embeddings for a query and a large corpus of documents, then finding the corpus entries whose embeddings are most similar to the query embedding. OpenAI's 'text-embedding-ada-002' and 'text-embedding-3-large' models are dedicated embedding models that produce 1536- or 3072-dimensional vectors optimized for retrieval.

import numpy as np
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def embed_texts(texts: list[str], model: str = "text-embedding-3-small") -> np.ndarray:
    """Embed a list of texts; returns (N, dim) float32 array."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(input=texts, model=model)
    vectors = [item.embedding for item in sorted(response.data, key=lambda x: x.index)]
    return np.array(vectors, dtype=np.float32)

def cosine_similarity(query_vec: np.ndarray, corpus_vecs: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """Compute cosine similarity between one query and many corpus vectors."""
    # Both should be pre-normalized (text-embedding-3 outputs are unit-normalized)
    return (corpus_vecs @ query_vec)  # (N,)

# Build a tiny knowledge base
knowledge_base = [
    "Python is a high-level, dynamically typed programming language.",
    "Tokenization splits raw text into a sequence of subword tokens.",
    "Transformers use self-attention to model dependencies between tokens.",
    "The context window limits how much text a model can process at once.",
    "BPE merges frequent character pairs to build a subword vocabulary.",
]

kb_embeddings = embed_texts(knowledge_base)   # (5, 1536)
print("Knowledge base embedding shape:", kb_embeddings.shape)

# Semantic search
query = "How does BPE work?"
query_embedding = embed_texts([query])[0]  # (1536,)

scores = cosine_similarity(query_embedding, kb_embeddings)
ranked = sorted(zip(scores, knowledge_base), reverse=True)

print(f"\nQuery: {query!r}")
print("Results:")
for score, doc in ranked:
    print(f"  [{score:.3f}] {doc[:70]}")

In production at scale, embedding similarity search is handled by vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector) that use approximate nearest-neighbor algorithms (HNSW, IVF) to search billions of vectors in sub-millisecond time. The key engineering challenge is not the embedding computation itself (which is batched and fast) but the indexing and querying of embedding vectors at scale.
Mistake 1: Comparing token embeddings directly across models

# WRONG: comparing embeddings from different models
from transformers import AutoModel, AutoTokenizer
import torch.nn.functional as F
import torch

# BERT and GPT-2 use completely different embedding spaces
bert_model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
gpt2_model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("gpt2")

bert_tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
gpt2_tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")

text = "Hello world"

def get_mean_emb(model, tokenizer, text):
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model(**inputs)
    return out.last_hidden_state[0].mean(dim=0)

bert_emb = get_mean_emb(bert_model, bert_tok, text)
gpt2_emb = get_mean_emb(gpt2_model, gpt2_tok, text)

# These vectors live in completely different spaces
# Cosine similarity is meaningless across model families:
# print(F.cosine_similarity(bert_emb, gpt2_emb, dim=0))  # meaningless number

# CORRECT: always compare embeddings from the same model
Mistake 2: Ignoring the attention mask when mean-pooling

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

texts = ["Short text", "A much longer sentence that will get padded"]
inputs = tokenizer(texts, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

hidden = outputs.last_hidden_state  # (2, max_len, 768)

# WRONG: mean over all positions including [PAD]
bad_emb = hidden.mean(dim=1)  # averages padding zeros into the embedding

# CORRECT: mask out padding positions before mean-pooling
mask = inputs["attention_mask"].unsqueeze(-1).float()  # (2, max_len, 1)
sum_hidden = (hidden * mask).sum(dim=1)                # (2, 768)
count = mask.sum(dim=1)                                 # (2, 1)
good_emb = sum_hidden / count                           # (2, 768)

print("Bad emb norm (sentence 1):", bad_emb[1].norm().item())   # lower, diluted
print("Good emb norm (sentence 1):", good_emb[1].norm().item()) # correct
Mistake 3: Randomly initializing embeddings for new tokens

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

new_tokens = ["[TOOL_CALL]", "[TOOL_RESULT]"]
tokenizer.add_special_tokens({"additional_special_tokens": new_tokens})

# WRONG: resize and use random initializations (default)
model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))
# New rows are ~random — the model produces garbage for these tokens
# until trained on substantial data

# BETTER: initialize from the mean of semantically related existing tokens
with torch.no_grad():
    tool_tokens = ["tool", "call", "function", "invoke"]
    tool_ids = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tool_tokens)
    tool_ids = [i for i in tool_ids if i != tokenizer.unk_token_id]
    mean_vec = model.embeddings.word_embeddings.weight[tool_ids].mean(dim=0)

    # Assign to the new token rows
    for tok in new_tokens:
        tid = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tok)
        model.embeddings.word_embeddings.weight[tid] = mean_vec

print("New token embeddings initialized from semantically related mean.")

Always use dedicated embedding models (e.g., text-embedding-3-small, text-embedding-ada-002, bge-large) for retrieval tasks rather than using the last hidden state of a generative model. Embedding models are trained with contrastive objectives that produce better retrieval embeddings.
Normalize embedding vectors to unit length before computing cosine similarity. Unit-normalized vectors make dot product equivalent to cosine similarity, enabling faster BLAS operations.

When adding new tokens, initialize their embeddings from the average of semantically related existing tokens rather than using random initialization. This dramatically reduces the amount of fine-tuning data needed.

Use BFloat16 (bf16) or Float16 (fp16) for embedding storage in production to halve memory usage with negligible quality impact. Most transformer models trained with bf16 produce embedding quality within 0.1% of float32.
Cache embeddings for static documents in your vector database. Recomputing embeddings on every request is wasteful — the corpus typically changes slowly.

For fine-tuning, selectively freeze the embedding layer if your dataset is small. The embedding matrix has the most parameters of any single layer; overfitting it can significantly hurt generalization.

The embedding lookup itself is a memory-bandwidth-bound operation: for a vocabulary of 50K tokens with d=768, the embedding matrix is 50,000 × 768 × 4 bytes ≈ 150 MB. For batch size 1, looking up 100 token embeddings reads 100 × 768 × 4 = 307 KB, but the GPU must load the embedding matrix pages from HBM, causing cache-miss latency.

For large-scale pretraining, the embedding layer accounts for a disproportionate share of memory and computation when the vocabulary is large. Tied weights reduce the memory footprint but not computation. Alternative strategies include:
. Used in ALBERT to reduce parameters.
Assign higher-dimensional embeddings to frequent tokens and lower-dimensional to rare tokens. Frequent tokens require richer representations; rare tokens need less capacity.
During training, only the embedding rows corresponding to tokens seen in the current batch receive gradient updates. This naturally focuses compute on frequent tokens.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Factored embedding (ALBERT-style) for large vocabularies
class FactoredEmbedding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, d_model: int, d_embed: int = 128):
        super().__init__()
        # d_embed << d_model saves parameters
        self.embed_small = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_embed)
        self.project_up = nn.Linear(d_embed, d_model, bias=False)

    def forward(self, ids: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        return self.project_up(self.embed_small(ids))

# Parameter count comparison
vocab_size = 30_000
d_model = 4096

standard_params = vocab_size * d_model
factored_params = vocab_size * 128 + 128 * d_model

print(f"Standard embedding params: {standard_params:,}")   # 122.88M
print(f"Factored embedding params: {factored_params:,}")   # 4.36M
print(f"Reduction: {standard_params / factored_params:.1f}x")
The standard embedding module. Supports padding_idx (one row is zeroed and never updated), max_norm, and sparse gradient updates for efficiency.
AutoModel.from_pretrained loads pretrained embedding matrices alongside the full model. The resize_token_embeddings method extends the vocabulary safely.
text-embedding-3-small and text-embedding-3-large produce high-quality embeddings for retrieval. Simple POST call, no local GPU needed.
Fine-tuned BERT models optimized for sentence-level embedding quality. Provides sentence_transformers.SentenceTransformer with encode() that handles batching, padding, and mean-pooling automatically.
Fast approximate nearest-neighbor library. Indexes billions of embedding vectors and retrieves the top-k similar in sub-millisecond time.
Managed vector databases built on HNSW or IVF indexing. Handle persistence, filtering, and horizontal scaling of embedding stores.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

# SBERT: production-grade sentence embeddings, no manual pooling needed
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")  # 384-dim, fast

texts = [
    "Tokenization splits text into subword units.",
    "Embeddings are dense vector representations of tokens.",
    "Context windows limit how much text a model can process.",
]
embeddings = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
print("Embedding shape:", embeddings.shape)  # (3, 384)

# Cosine similarity matrix
sim_matrix = embeddings @ embeddings.T
print("\nSimilarity matrix:")
for i, ti in enumerate(texts):
    for j, tj in enumerate(texts):
        if j > i:
            print(f"  Sent{i} ↔ Sent{j}: {sim_matrix[i,j]:.3f}")

Given an embedding vector, it is theoretically possible to recover approximate input text (especially with smaller models or large vocabularies). If embedding vectors are shared with third parties, this may leak private input text. Treat embeddings as sensitive data in high-privacy contexts.

Small changes to input text can cause large shifts in the embedding space, leading to dramatically different model behavior. Attackers can craft inputs that look benign to humans but map to adversarial regions of the embedding space.

Large models can memorize specific training examples in their embedding weights, particularly for rare tokens that appear in only a few documents. Fine-tuning on proprietary data may leak that data via embedding similarity searches.
The same surface string may receive different token IDs across tokenizer versions. Never hardcode token IDs as magic numbers in application logic; always resolve them via the tokenizer object.

Token IDs are like phone numbers: they are arbitrary integer identifiers with no intrinsic meaning, and their only purpose is to look up an entry in a directory. The directory is the embedding matrix. Calling a phone number doesn't tell you anything about the person at the other end; you need to make the call (do the lookup) to get the actual information.

Embeddings are the phone book entries: rich, multi-dimensional descriptions of each token's meaning, context, and relationships. The geometry of the embedding space encodes semantic relationships: similar words are near each other; words with similar roles in sentences are in similar regions; antonyms are separated by a consistent directional offset.

Weight tying is like using the same phonebook for both input and output: when a model generates an output token, it is essentially asking 'which phonebook entry best matches the hidden state I computed?' and returning the ID of that entry. Using the same phonebook for input (lookup) and output (matching) forces the model to represent tokens consistently — a token that is a good input for one meaning must also be a good output prediction for that same meaning.
Token IDs are compact integer indices into the vocabulary; embeddings are the dense float vectors retrieved by those IDs from the embedding matrix E ∈ ℝ^(|V|×d).
The embedding matrix is the model's primary interface with language: it maps discrete symbols to continuous geometry and is updated by gradient descent to encode semantic and syntactic relationships.
Weight tying shares the embedding matrix between input lookup and output projection, halving vocabulary-related parameters and improving perplexity on most benchmarks.

Embedding-based similarity (cosine distance) captures semantic relationships that are invisible in token ID space — two tokens with IDs 1 and 50000 may be semantically identical, while IDs 1 and 2 may be unrelated.
Never compare embeddings across different models or tokenizers; the embedding spaces are different and cosine similarities between them are meaningless.
Always apply the attention mask when mean-pooling token embeddings in batches with padding; including [PAD] positions dilutes the resulting sentence embedding.

For production retrieval, use dedicated embedding models (SBERT, text-embedding-3) rather than generative model hidden states; their contrastive training objectives produce superior retrieval quality.
